title: Arjuna Vishada Yoga
subtitle: The Yoga of Arjuna's Dejection
summary: On the battlefield of Kurukshetra, the blind king Dhritarashtra asks his advisor Sanjaya (gifted with clairvoyant sight) to describe the scene. Both armies array for war and sound their conches. Arjuna asks Krishna to drive his chariot between the two armies so he can see who he must fight — and is overwhelmed with grief and doubt at the sight of his own family, teachers, and friends arrayed for battle on both sides.
note: The text below is a concise plain-English paraphrase of each verse for study purposes, not a literal scholarly translation. Verse numbering follows the standard 47-verse division used in most editions.

verses.speaker: Dhritarashtra
verses.text: Dhritarashtra asked Sanjaya: assembled on the sacred field of Kurukshetra, eager to fight, what did my sons and the sons of Pandu do?

verses.speaker: Sanjaya
verses.text: Sanjaya replied: having seen the Pandava army arrayed for battle, King Duryodhana approached his teacher Drona and spoke.

verses.speaker: Duryodhana
verses.text: Duryodhana said: Behold, teacher, this mighty army of the sons of Pandu, arrayed by your own skilled pupil, the son of Drupada.

verses.speaker: Duryodhana
verses.text: Here are heroes and mighty archers equal to Bhima and Arjuna in battle — Yuyudhana, Virata, and the great warrior Drupada.

verses.speaker: Duryodhana
verses.text: Also Dhrishtaketu, Chekitana, the valiant king of Kashi, Purujit, Kuntibhoja, and Shaibya, the best of men.

verses.speaker: Duryodhana
verses.text: The strong Yudhamanyu, the brave Uttamaujas, the son of Subhadra, and the sons of Draupadi — all great chariot-warriors.

verses.speaker: Duryodhana
verses.text: Duryodhana continued: know also, O best of the twice-born, the distinguished leaders of my own army — let me name them for you.

verses.speaker: Duryodhana
verses.text: Yourself, Bhishma, Karna, Kripa ever victorious in battle, Ashwatthama, Vikarna, and the son of Somadatta.

verses.speaker: Duryodhana
verses.text: And many other heroes ready to give up their lives for my sake, skilled in warfare and equipped with many kinds of weapons.

verses.speaker: Duryodhana
verses.text: Our strength, protected as it is by Bhishma, seems boundless; their strength, protected by Bhima, seems limited.

verses.speaker: Duryodhana
verses.text: Therefore let all of you, stationed in your respective positions on every front, guard and support Bhishma above all.

verses.speaker: Sanjaya
verses.text: Bhishma, the valiant elder of the Kurus, roared a lion's roar and blew his conch loudly, gladdening Duryodhana's heart.

verses.speaker: Sanjaya
verses.text: Then conches, kettledrums, cymbals, and horns suddenly blared out together, and the combined sound was tumultuous.

verses.speaker: Sanjaya
verses.text: Then Krishna and Arjuna, standing in their magnificent chariot yoked with white horses, blew their divine conches.

verses.speaker: Sanjaya
verses.text: Krishna blew his conch Panchajanya; Arjuna blew Devadatta; and Bhima, the wolf-bellied, blew his great conch Paundra.

verses.speaker: Sanjaya
verses.text: King Yudhishthira, son of Kunti, blew Anantavijaya; Nakula and Sahadeva blew Sughosha and Manipushpaka.

verses.speaker: Sanjaya
verses.text: The king of Kashi, the great archer Shikhandi, Dhrishtadyumna, Virata, and the unconquered Satyaki also blew their conches.

verses.speaker: Sanjaya
verses.text: Drupada, the sons of Draupadi, and the mighty-armed son of Subhadra all blew their conches from every side.

verses.speaker: Sanjaya
verses.text: That tumultuous sound, echoing through heaven and earth, tore at the hearts of Dhritarashtra's sons.

verses.speaker: Sanjaya
verses.text: Then Arjuna, seeing Dhritarashtra's sons arrayed and ready, raised his bow as the clash of arms was about to begin.

verses.speaker: Arjuna
verses.text: Arjuna said to Krishna: place my chariot between the two armies.

verses.speaker: Arjuna
verses.text: So that I may see those assembled here, eager for battle, with whom I must contend in this great war.

verses.speaker: Arjuna
verses.text: I wish to see those gathered here to fight, intending to please the ill-intentioned son of Dhritarashtra.

verses.speaker: Sanjaya
verses.text: Sanjaya said: thus addressed by Arjuna, Krishna drove the magnificent chariot between the two armies.

verses.speaker: Sanjaya
verses.text: In front of Bhishma, Drona, and all the assembled rulers, Krishna said: Arjuna, behold these Kurus gathered here.

verses.speaker: Sanjaya
verses.text: There Arjuna saw, standing in both armies, fathers, grandfathers, teachers, uncles, brothers, sons, grandsons, and friends.

verses.speaker: Sanjaya
verses.text: He also saw fathers-in-law and companions in both armies. Seeing all these kinsmen arrayed, Arjuna was overcome with deep compassion.

verses.speaker: Arjuna
verses.text: Arjuna said, filled with sorrow: Krishna, seeing my own people here, arrayed and eager to fight, my limbs give way and my mouth goes dry.

verses.speaker: Arjuna
verses.text: My body trembles, my hair stands on end, my bow Gandiva slips from my hand, and my skin feels as if it is burning.

verses.speaker: Arjuna
verses.text: I am unable to stand steady; my mind is reeling. I see only unfavorable omens, Krishna.

verses.speaker: Arjuna
verses.text: I see no good in killing my own kinsmen in this battle. I have no desire for victory, kingdom, or pleasures, Krishna.

verses.speaker: Arjuna
verses.text: What use is a kingdom to us, Govinda, or enjoyment, or even life itself?

verses.speaker: Arjuna
verses.text: Those for whose sake we would want kingdom, enjoyments, and pleasures are themselves standing here, ready to give up their wealth and lives.

verses.speaker: Arjuna
verses.text: Teachers, fathers, sons, grandfathers, uncles, fathers-in-law, grandsons, brothers-in-law, and other relatives stand arrayed before me.

verses.speaker: Arjuna
verses.text: I do not wish to kill them, even if I myself were to be killed, Krishna — not for rule over the three worlds, let alone for this earth.

verses.speaker: Arjuna
verses.text: What joy could we find, Krishna, in killing the sons of Dhritarashtra? Only sin would come to us by slaying these aggressors.

verses.speaker: Arjuna
verses.text: Therefore we should not kill our own kinsmen, the sons of Dhritarashtra — for how could we be happy after killing our own family, Krishna?

verses.speaker: Arjuna
verses.text: Even if they, blinded by greed, see no wrong in destroying a family or being treacherous to friends, why should we not know better?

verses.speaker: Arjuna
verses.text: Why should we not turn away from this evil act, when we clearly understand the harm caused by destroying a family?

verses.speaker: Arjuna
verses.text: With the destruction of a family, its long-standing traditions and values collapse, and when those are lost, disorder overtakes the whole family.

verses.speaker: Arjuna
verses.text: When disorder prevails, it corrupts the wider community, causing confusion and social harm that spreads beyond the immediate family.

verses.speaker: Arjuna
verses.text: Such confusion drags both the family and its destroyers down, harming the memory and legacy of ancestors who came before.

verses.speaker: Arjuna
verses.text: By the misdeeds of those who destroy a family, long-standing communal and family traditions are ruined for good.

verses.speaker: Arjuna
verses.text: We have heard, Krishna, that people whose family traditions are destroyed are condemned to live with the weight of that loss indefinitely.

verses.speaker: Arjuna
verses.text: Alas, what a grave wrong we have resolved to commit — driven by the desire for a kingdom and its pleasures, we are prepared to kill our own kin.

verses.speaker: Arjuna
verses.text: It would be better for me if the sons of Dhritarashtra, weapons in hand, killed me unarmed and unresisting on this battlefield.

verses.speaker: Sanjaya
verses.text: Sanjaya said: having spoken thus on the battlefield, Arjuna cast aside his bow and arrows and sank down onto the seat of his chariot, his mind overwhelmed with grief.
